text: During the robotic sleeve gastrectomy, the left lobe of the liver is retracted using the Dima Havaleshko liver suspension stitch. The robotic setup includes Cartier instruments in arms one and four, a camera in arm two, and a needle driver or vessel sealer in arm three. The vessel sealer marks six centimeters from the pylorus and is used to detach the stomach from the gastroepiploic vessels, working proximally to avoid thermal injury. Arms one and four retract while arm three with the vessel sealer detaches attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach.

The surgeon carefully elevates the fundus and dissects the posterior attachments of the stomach near the spleen, pancreas, and fat pad using a vessel sealer. The stomach is retracted cephalad, and attachments to the left cruciate of the diaphragm are cleared. A 36 French bougie is passed by the anesthesia team, and the fat pad is dissected medially towards the bougie's margin. The liver suture retractor holds the fat pad out of the way, allowing for further dissection to ensure uniform stomach thickness for stapler passage. The bougie is advanced to the antrum, guiding the dissection of posterior attachments as needed.

The camera is repositioned to arm three, while arm two holds a twelve millimeter port for the stapler. The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the antrum of the stomach at the pre-marked point near the pylorus, ensuring adequate distance from the incisura angularis to prevent an hourglass deformity. The bougie is aligned on the lesser curve of the stomach to maintain this distance during proximal fundus resection. The stomach is divided, excess staples are removed, and the stapler is reloaded. The third fire of the blue load stapler is aligned with the bougie as the surgeon continues to resect the stomach cephalad, checking both anteriorly and posteriorly to confirm the correct amount of stomach is resected.

Arm four retracts the fundus to create space for the stapler's final application. The anterior and posterior dimensions of the stomach are equalized, and the distance from the gastroesophageal junction is checked to ensure it is not encroached upon. The position of the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction is verified before clamping and firing the stapler. The stomach is divided between the specimen and the sleeve stomach using the cold scissors function of the Vessel sealer.

The surgeon introduces a 3-0 PDS suture on an SH-1 needle into the abdomen to begin oversewing the sleeve staple line. The proximal corner is dunked to start the imbrication, and suturing proceeds caudally, approximating the serosa of the anterior and posterior stomach. A staple is removed to improve imbrication. Care is taken to avoid injuring large vessels during suturing. The distal antrum undergoes omental gastropexy to stabilize the sleeve and prevent twisting. The running suture is tightened and secured, resulting in a smooth, tapered sleeve gastrectomy without kinking.
The surgical table is flattened for an endoscopic leak test, showing no visible extravasation of carbon dioxide from the sleeve gastrectomy site.
